did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), specifically five mainstream deep learning algorithms including Inception-ResNet-v2, to classify six common facial skin diseases (seborrheic keratosis, actinic keratosis, rosacea, lupus erythematosus, basal cell carcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma) based on clinical images from the Xiangya-Derm dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the construction of a new dataset (2,656 facial images from the Xiangya-Derm dataset) and the performance evaluation of five different CNN algorithms (including Inception-ResNet-v2) for classifying six facial skin diseases. It reports specific quantitative results (e.g., 92.9% recall for LE) and conclusions derived from these experiments, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Studies on Different CNN Algorithms for Face Skin Disease Classification Based on Clinical Images
brief_summary: This study evaluated five mainstream Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) algorithms for classifying six common facial skin diseases: seborrheic keratosis, actinic keratosis, rosacea, lupus erythematosus, basal cell carcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma. The researchers constructed a dataset of 2,656 facial images from the Xiangya-Derm clinical image repository, which was rigorously reviewed by dermatologists. The models were trained and tested on facial images, with additional experiments conducted using transfer learning from an independent dataset of images from other body parts. The best-performing model, Inception-ResNet-v2, achieved a mean recall of 77.0% and precision of 70.8% on the test set, with specific recalls of 92.9% for lupus erythematosus and 89.2% for basal cell carcinoma. The results demonstrated that CNNs can effectively diagnose facial skin diseases and that transfer learning improves performance, though overall accuracy requires further improvement for clinical deployment.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the six common facial skin diseases studied in the classification task. Included in the Xiangya-Derm derived dataset of 2,656 facial images. Misclassification with AK noted due to similar clinical manifestations.

all_task_types.target: Six common facial skin disease categories (seborrheic keratosis, actinic keratosis, rosacea, lupus erythematosus, basal cell carcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of facial skin diseases using clinical images. The study evaluated five CNN algorithms (including Inception-ResNet-v2) to predict discrete diagnostic labels. Performance was measured using recall, precision, and confusion matrices for the six specific disease classes.

datasets_used.name: Xiangya-Derm (Facial Subset)
datasets_used.description: A subset of the Xiangya-Derm dataset, described as China's largest clinical image dataset of skin diseases, consisting of facial images from patients at Xiangya Hospital, Central South University. Images were captured by digital camera and rigorously reviewed by at least three experienced dermatologists. The dataset was split by patient to prevent data leakage between training and testing.

use.training: Used for training five CNN algorithms (Inception-ResNet-v2, ResNet-50, VGG-16, Xception, DenseNet-121) to classify facial skin diseases.
use.testing: Used for testing the performance of the models, specifically evaluating recall and precision for the six disease classes.
use.other: The parent dataset (Xiangya-Derm) contains 150,223 images from 543 diseases, but only the 2,656 facial images were used for this study's primary classification task.

datasets_used.name: Xiangya-Derm (Non-Facial Subset)
datasets_used.description: An independent dataset derived from the same Xiangya-Derm repository, consisting of images of the same six disease types but from other body parts (non-facial). Used to perform transfer learning.

use.training: Used for pre-training (transfer learning) to initialize the models before fine-tuning on the facial dataset.
use.other: Used to evaluate the impact of transfer learning on model performance compared to models trained only on facial images.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between models were based on descriptive statistics such as recall, precision, and confusion matrices.

Xiangya-Derm (Facial Subset) - No Transf.description: Results for five CNN models trained and tested on facial images without pre-training on non-facial data. The test set contained 388 images.

Inception-ResNet-v2.notes: Achieved the highest average precision among the five models.

Xiangya-Derm (Facial Subset) - No Transf.notes: The recall for Actinic Keratosis (AK) was only 54.1% for the best-performing model (Inception-ResNet-v2). Misclassifications between SK and AK were noted due to similar clinical manifestations and sample imbalance.

Xiangya-Derm (Facial Subset) - With Tran.description: Results for five CNN models pre-trained on non-facial images from the Xiangya-Derm dataset and then fine-tuned on facial images. The test set contained 388 images.

Inception-ResNet-v2.notes: Best performing model. Achieved highest recalls for LE, BCC, and SK. Mean recall and precision reached 77.0% and 70.8% respectively.

Xiangya-Derm (Facial Subset) - With Tran.notes: Models using transfer learning achieved higher average precision and recall for almost all structures compared to those trained only on facial images. The performance of the current network structure was satisfactory for some diseases but overall performance requires improvement.

architecture_used.main_model: Five mainstream CNN algorithms: Inception-ResNet-v2, ResNet-50, Inception-v3, DenseNet-121, and Xception.
architecture_used.pretraining: All five models were pretrained on ImageNet. Additionally, transfer learning was performed by pretraining models on an independent dataset of the same six disease types from other body parts (non-facial) before fine-tuning on the facial dataset.

architecture_details.general_modifications: The basic structure from the original papers was maintained. The first fully connected layer behind the last convolutional layer was replaced with global average pooling and a 1x1 convolution to reduce parameters and maintain spatial information. A 1024-d fully connected layer was used in each network.
architecture_details.ResNet-50: Adds connections between shallow and deep layers to transmit information and increase gradient propagation to shallow layers during backpropagation.
architecture_details.Inception-v3: Uses inception blocks with different kernel shapes to improve width diversity and receptive field scale diversity.
architecture_details.DenseNet-121: Adds connections between each two layers; output feature maps of each layer are used as input for all subsequent layers to reuse features.
architecture_details.Xception: Updated version of Inception using depthwise separable convolution to decouple spatial correlations and cross-channel correlations.
architecture_details.Inception-ResNet-v2: Combination of Inception and ResNet structures; adds residual connections to the Inception network to train deeper networks while maintaining scale diversity.

training_details.loss_function: Cost function with different weights for different diseases to address data imbalance. Softmax or logistic regression classifier used for output.

classification_or_output_head.output: 6 confidence outputs for the six facial skin diseases.

architecture_used.approximate_parameters: Not explicitly reported. The paper notes that DenseNet improves performance with fewer parameters and that global average pooling/1x1 convolution was used to reduce parameters in all models.

preprocessing_techniques: Random reverse (likely referring to random flipping/mirroring)
Use of different weights in the cost function to address data imbalance (class weighting)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied as part of the preprocessing pipeline for all images before training.
Applied as part of the preprocessing pipeline for all images before training.
Used different weights in the cost function for different diseases to address data imbalance, rather than traditional sampling-based augmentation.

conclusions: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have the ability to recognize and classify six common facial skin diseases (seborrheic keratosis, actinic keratosis, rosacea, lupus erythematosus, basal cell carcinoma, and squamous cell carcinoma) based on clinical images.

Transfer learning using an independent dataset of the same disease types from other body parts improves model performance, achieving higher average precision and recall compared to models trained only on facial images.
The Inception-ResNet-v2 model achieved the best performance among the five tested CNN algorithms, with a mean recall of 77.0% and mean precision of 70.8% on the test set.
Specific disease recalls for the best model (Inception-ResNet-v2) were 92.9% for lupus erythematosus, 89.2% for basal cell carcinoma, and 84.3% for seborrheic keratosis.
Actinic keratosis (AK) had the lowest recall (54.1%) due to similar clinical manifestations with seborrheic keratosis (SK) and sample imbalance in the dataset.

Different models should be used to diagnose diseases on different body parts, as there are significant differences between images of different body parts, although symptom differences for specific diseases may not be obvious.
A more reasonable network structure can improve the performance of the model.
The overall performance of the current network structure requires further improvement for clinical deployment, as it is only satisfactory for some diseases.
Deep learning models' outputs are hard to explain; visualization techniques like grad-CAM showed that CNNs generally make predictions based on features learned from lesion areas.
The application of artificial intelligence in dermatology is currently insufficient, and datasets need improvement in both quantity and quality.
Specialized improvements are needed for CNN-based techniques to be practically used by people for daily face skin health checks.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a single-center dataset (Xiangya-Derm) from China, which may introduce population and geographic bias, limiting generalizability to other ethnicities or clinical settings.

The dataset exhibits significant class imbalance, particularly with a significantly higher number of Seborrheic Keratosis (SK) images compared to Actinic Keratosis (AK), contributing to poor performance for AK.

Actinic Keratosis (AK) had the lowest recall (54.1%) due to similar clinical manifestations with SK and sample imbalance, highlighting a critical limitation in distinguishing benign from potentially malignant lesions.
The overall mean recall (77.0%) and precision (70.8%) are insufficient for direct clinical deployment without further improvement, as noted by the authors.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for performance comparisons between models, relying instead on descriptive statistics.
Data leakage risk was mitigated by splitting the training and test sets by patient rather than by image, ensuring images from the same patient did not appear in both sets.
The study lacks external validation on independent datasets from different institutions or populations.
The dataset is not yet publicly available, limiting reproducibility and independent verification of the results.
The study focuses only on six specific facial skin diseases, excluding other common conditions or complex presentations, which limits the scope of the model's applicability.
The authors acknowledge that deep learning models are 'hard to explain,' though they used grad-CAM to show that predictions were based on lesion areas rather than background artifacts.
